narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 54% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your bottom-decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 school grades—far below the typical student's mid-range performance—along with your LSAT score of 22, which is significantly lower than the average of around 37. You stand out positively with an undergraduate GPA of 3.8, well above the usual 3.2, and you're planning full-time studies like most applicants, but other factors weighed more heavily. The model's decision was most strongly influenced by your Year 3 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction firmly toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom tier, reinforcing the same concern about early academic struggles. Your low LSAT score came next in importance, further tipping the scales toward failure. Your lower-middle family income bracket played a notable role too, followed by your Native American background, which is less common in the dataset where most students are White. Even your strong undergraduate GPA and female gender had some influence toward the failure prediction, though they ranked lower in impact, as did your full-time status. Compared to typical students—who have stronger high school deciles, higher LSATs, and often higher family incomes—your profile showed more risk factors pulling in the same direction. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can realistically address, like retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, as it's a key changeable factor that heavily swayed this outcome. Building a stronger academic record in any upcoming programs could also help shift future predictions. We encourage you to reapply after targeting these areas—you have real strengths like your GPA that could shine brighter with those improvements. Keep pushing forward; many students overcome similar hurdles.
